question: Explain the fundamental purpose and advantage of creating a multilevel class hierarchy in Object-Oriented Programming.
expectedPoints: To model a real-world 'is-a-kind-of' relationship, promoting a more natural and logical organization of classes.
To enable code reusability through inheritance, allowing subclasses to inherit fields and methods from their parent classes.
To support polymorphism, where a reference of a superclass type can be used to refer to an object of a subclass, facilitating flexible and extensible code.

question: Describe the process of constructor calling in a three-level class hierarchy (e.g., Grandparent -> Parent -> Child) during object instantiation. What is the role of the 'super' keyword in this process?
expectedPoints: The constructor call chain begins with the constructor of the instantiated class (e.g., Child).
The first line of a constructor, if not explicitly written, is an implicit call to super(), invoking the no-argument constructor of its immediate superclass (e.g., Parent).
This process continues up the hierarchy (to Grandparent) until the Object class constructor is reached, ensuring all ancestors are properly initialized before the descendant.
The 'super' keyword is used to explicitly call a specific constructor in the immediate superclass, which must be the first statement in the subclass constructor.

question: In a multilevel hierarchy, if a subclass overrides a method from its superclass, how does this affect the method resolution when the method is called on an object reference of the superclass type?
expectedPoints: Java uses dynamic method dispatch (runtime polymorphism). The actual version of the method that is executed is determined by the type of the object being referred to, not the type of the reference variable.
Therefore, if a superclass reference points to a subclass object, and the subclass has overridden the method, the overridden method in the subclass is the one that will be called.
